stem: Consider the four vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) \[ \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\ [[s]],\ [[t]]),\quad \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[p]],\ [[m22]],\ [[m23]]),\quad \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[q]],\ [[m32]],\ [[m33]]),\quad \mathbf{v}_4 = ([[v4a]],\ [[v4b]],\ [[v4c]]). \] What is the largest number of these vectors that form a linearly independent set?
solution: The largest independent subset has size equal to the rank of the matrix formed by these vectors, which is \( [[answer]] \). (Since four vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) are always dependent, the answer is at most \( 3 \).)
distractor_rationales: All four vectors cannot be independent: four vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) are always dependent.
This undercounts the size of a maximal independent subset by one.
This overcounts the size of a maximal independent subset by one.
This assumes the vectors span all of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) without checking.
This treats every vector as redundant.
This undercounts the maximal independent subset.
This miscounts the number of independent vectors.
This counts the redundant vectors instead of the independent ones.

stem: Consider the three vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \): \[ \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\ [[s]],\ [[t]]),\quad \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[p]],\ [[m22]],\ [[m23]]),\quad \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[q]],\ [[m32]],\ [[m33]]). \] What is the rank of the matrix whose rows are these three vectors? (They form a basis of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) precisely when this rank is as large as possible.)
solution: Row-reducing the \( 3 \times 3 \) matrix with rows \( \mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3 \) shows its rank is \( [[answer]] \); the vectors form a basis of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) exactly when this rank equals \( 3 \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes any three vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) automatically form a basis, without checking for dependence.
This assumes the vectors are always dependent, which is not guaranteed.
This undercounts the rank by one.
This overcounts the rank by one, which cannot exceed the number of rows or columns.
This is a plausible but unverified rank value.
This treats the vectors as spanning only a line.
This undercounts the rank by two.
This subtracts the rank from the ambient dimension instead of reporting the rank itself.

stem: Consider the three vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^4 \): \[ \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\ [[s]],\ [[t]],\ [[w]]),\quad \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[p]],\ [[m22]],\ [[m23]],\ [[m24]]),\quad \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[q]],\ [[m32]],\ [[m33]],\ [[m34]]). \] What is the dimension of the subspace spanned by \( \{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3\} \)?
solution: The dimension of the span of \( \mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3 \) equals the rank of the \( 3 \times 4 \) matrix they form, which is \( [[answer]] \). Three vectors can never span more than a \( 3 \)-dimensional subspace, even inside \( \mathbb{R}^4 \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes three vectors can span all of the ambient \( 4 \)-dimensional space.
This wrongly assumes the vectors are always fully dependent.
This undercounts the dimension of the span by one.
This overcounts the dimension of the span, which cannot exceed the number of vectors.
This undercounts the dimension of the span by two.
This assumes the vectors span only a line.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\ [[a]],\ [[b]]) \), \( \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[c]],\ [[d]],\ [[e]]) \), and \( \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[f]],\ [[g]],\ t) \), where \( t \) is a real parameter. For what value of \( t \) are \( \mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3 \) linearly dependent (equivalently, for what \( t \) do they fail to form a basis of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \))?
solution: The vectors are linearly dependent exactly when \( \det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] & [[e]] \\ [[f]] & [[g]] & t \end{bmatrix} = 0 \). Expanding along the first row gives a linear equation in \( t \), whose solution is \( t = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the negative of the correct value, from a sign error in the cofactor expansion.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This mistakes the given entry \( g \) for the dependence-causing value of \( t \).
This mistakes the given entry \( f \) for the dependence-causing value of \( t \).

stem: The vectors \( \mathbf{v}_1 = ([[a]],\ [[d]],\ [[g]]) \), \( \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[b]],\ [[e]],\ [[h]]) \), \( \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[c]],\ [[f]],\ [[i]]) \) form a basis for \( \mathbb{R}^3 \). The vector \( \mathbf{w} = ([[w1]],\ [[w2]],\ [[w3]]) \) is written as \( \mathbf{w} = x\mathbf{v}_1 + y\mathbf{v}_2 + z\mathbf{v}_3 \). What is the value of \( x \)?
solution: Writing \( \mathbf{w} = x\mathbf{v}_1+y\mathbf{v}_2+z\mathbf{v}_3 \) componentwise gives the linear system \( [[a]]x+[[b]]y+[[c]]z=[[w1]] \), \( [[d]]x+[[e]]y+[[f]]z=[[w2]] \), \( [[g]]x+[[h]]y+[[i]]z=[[w3]] \). Solving this system for \( x \) gives \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakes the first coordinate of \( \mathbf{w} \) itself for the coefficient \( x \).
This is the negative of the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This combines the wrong coordinates of \( \mathbf{w} \) instead of solving the system.
This sums the coordinates of \( \mathbf{w} \) instead of solving the system.

stem: The vectors \( \mathbf{v}_1 = ([[a]],\ [[b]],\ [[c]]) \) and \( \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[d]],\ [[e]],\ [[f]]) \) span a two-dimensional subspace of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \). For what value of \( t \) does the vector \( \mathbf{w} = ([[w1]],\ [[w2]],\ t) \) lie in this subspace, i.e. in the span of \( \mathbf{v}_1 \) and \( \mathbf{v}_2 \)?
solution: Solving \( [[a]]x+[[d]]y=[[w1]] \) and \( [[b]]x+[[e]]y=[[w2]] \) for \( x, y \) (unique since \( \mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2 \) are independent), then substituting into \( [[c]]x+[[f]]y=t \) gives \( t = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This sums the two given coordinates of \( \mathbf{w} \) instead of solving the system.
This sums the third-coordinate entries of \( \mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2 \) instead of solving for the combination.
This is the negative of the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This subtracts the two given coordinates of \( \mathbf{w} \) instead of solving the system.
